upperAir: KOAK sounding shows a moist, nearly saturated boundary layer at 850mb (5.6°C/5.1°C spread of only 0.5°C) with overcast skies — thermal development is severely limited and the BL top is shallow (~1450m MSL). The surface-to-850mb lapse rate is 6.5°C/km, right at moist adiabatic, meaning convective instability is minimal. Upper winds veer from S at low levels to SSW at 700mb at 23 kts, with 500mb winds at 33 kts SSW — moderate shear aloft but thermal ceiling is effectively capped well below ridge-top for most sites.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for Bay Area. KSFO: 200/10G19KT, BKN028-BKN180, recent rain (RAE02), altimeter 29.86. KHAF: 190-200/12G20KT, SCT-BKN clouds 050-060ft, near-saturation dewpoint spread (17/17 at 2015Z) indicating thick marine layer persisting at the coast. KRHV: 210/12G19KT, BKN060, temp 21°C — inland clearing better but gusts active. KAPC: recent rain (RAE2059), 190/11KT, FEW018/SCT050 — cloud base marginal. KWVI: 250-260/10-11G21KT, FEW050/BKN080 — westerly flow at Watsonville. NDBC 46012 (Half Moon Bay): SST 14.0°C, wave period 13s — cold SST and long-period swell indicate a robust marine influence; expect marine layer persistence at coastal sites.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — thermal/ridge site, S-SW facing upper launch
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin 1750 works on thermals from the heated Santa Clara Valley floor triggering lift off the sunlit south-facing ridge. Ideal wind is SSW to S at 8–15 kts on the upper launch. Sea breeze arrival from the bay typically reinforces afternoon flow but can become too strong. Thermal ceiling and cloud base must be above launch for viable soaring.
sites.todaySetup: The decisive problem today is 100% cloud cover persisting all day per both HRRR and GFS, corroborated by KRHV (BKN060) and KAPC (post-rain clouds), which will suppress solar heating and kill thermal development. Despite moderate S-SSW surface winds of 10–14 kts that are technically on-direction for the upper launch, there is no thermal engine. CAPE values are elevated (430–920 J/kg) suggesting atmospheric instability if heating were to occur, but overcast skies prevent ground heating. BL depth remains shallow at 265–765m early, only reaching ~1070m by late afternoon — barely above the 1750ft (533m) launch elevation. The 80m wind at ridge level climbs to 15–17 kts by afternoon, which could offer marginal ridge soaring, but with no thermal cycle and gusty, variable conditions, there is no viable window.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: SSW 7–10 kts at surface, 9–14 kts at 80m. 100% cloud cover, BL only 265–380m. No thermal activity possible; conditions are damp and overcast.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds build to 10–14 kts SSW, 80m winds 12–17 kts. CAPE rising to 620–920 J/kg but cloud cover still 91–100%. BL expands to 685–735m but solar input absent — thermals cannot fire.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds steady 12–14 kts SSW, gusts implied by KRHV at 19 kts. Cloud coverage fluctuates (29–100%) with brief possible partial breaks around 4 PM, but BL depth and marginal thermal potential remain insufficient for consistent soaring.

verdict.hazards: 100% overcast suppressing thermals, gusty ridge-level winds 15–17 kts at 80m, post-rain instability, low BL ceiling

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.howItWorks: The 600ft lower launch is a training hill that works on gentle SSW valley breezes and weak thermals from the warming valley floor. Ideal wind is S-SSW at 5–12 kts. Used for low-airtime flights and student training; very sensitive to overdeveloped conditions or gusty air.
sites.todaySetup: Same overcast story as the upper launch, but more critical here because thermal lift is essentially zero on a cloudy day at this low elevation. The BL barely exceeds the launch height (600ft/183m) before 11 AM, and cloud cover is 100% all day. Surface winds at the lower launch are 3–13 kts SSW — usable direction but the gusty afternoon flow (KRHV 12G19KT) makes this unsuitable for training. KWVI gusts at 21 kts signal the sea breeze push strengthening from the south. No window appropriate even for students.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Very light S winds 3–9 kts at surface. Overcast 100%, BL only 295–460m. Too light and too cloudy for any meaningful flight; no thermal gradient.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds pick up to 9–13 kts SSW but 100% cloud cover persists. BL expanding to 570–905m. Gusty character developing — not appropriate for training hill operations.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 10–13 kts SSW with gusts implied. Too gusty and turbulent for the lower launch; 100% overcast eliminates any thermal rewards.

verdict.hazards: Full overcast eliminating thermals, afternoon gusts to 19+ kts, post-rain instability in the valley

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal bluff, WNW-facing ridge site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge soaring site powered by the Pacific sea breeze. It needs W to NW wind at 10–20 kts blowing onshore to produce reliable lift along the coastal bluff. Winds below 8 kts produce insufficient lift; above 25 kts it becomes dangerously strong. The marine layer can lower cloud base into the ridge, making it unflyable.
sites.todaySetup: The primary problem at Mussel Rock today is the wind direction and speed being sub-optimal through much of the day. KHAF METARs show S-SSW flow at 12–15G20KT — this is more south than the ideal west component needed for Mussel Rock's WNW-facing bluff. The Half Moon Bay buoy SST is cold at 14°C with a 13-second wave period, indicating a solid marine layer. Cloud base at KHAF is 050–060ft with near-saturated conditions, and KSFO shows BKN028 — the marine layer is thick and will likely sit on or below the ridge top through morning. Afternoon shows a slight wind shift toward W-SW (220–232°) with speeds building to 8–10 kts, but this is marginal for the ridge geometry and the 80m winds remain moderate at 10–15 kts. The overall picture is marginal to no-fly: variable, mostly southerly low-level flow, persistent overcast, and a marine layer that is not clearly burning off.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds SE-S at 3–6 kts — wrong direction and too light for ridge lift. 99–100% cloud cover with marine layer sitting low per KHAF. Not flyable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind direction variable, swinging between SSW and WSW (203–245°), speeds 1.5–8 kts. Highly inconsistent — brief lulls near 1.5 kts at 1 PM are a red flag for rotor or compression. Still 100% overcast.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds gradually shift to WSW-SW (218–232°) and build to 8–10 kts by 5–7 PM. This is the most viable window but still marginal — 80m winds at 12–16 kts and variable cloud. P3+ only for late afternoon probing.

verdict.hazards: Wrong wind direction (S instead of W-NW) most of day, marine layer at ridge level, near-calm lull at 1 PM suggesting compression/rotor risk, cold SST supporting persistent marine layer

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — thermal/ridge site, W-SW facing, 2517ft summit
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak relies on a combination of valley thermals from the Fremont/Tri-Valley area and afternoon sea breeze-driven ridge lift from the W-SW. Ideal conditions are SSW-WSW at 8–15 kts with a clear or partly cloudy sky allowing solar heating to drive thermals above the 2517ft summit. Wind over 20 kts at ridge level becomes dangerous.
sites.todaySetup: Like Ed Levin, Mission Peak is a thermal site and the 100% cloud cover today is the flight-killer. Despite favorable SSW flow at 10–14 kts — right direction for the site — without solar heating there are no thermals to work. The BL depth starting at just 265m and only reaching 765–1040m by late afternoon is barely above the valley floor, well short of the ~767m summit. CAPE values of 430–920 J/kg indicate potential instability if sun were to break through, but KRHV (BKN060) and KAPC (recent rain, FEW018) confirm the inland cloud deck is holding. Ridge-level winds at 80m build to 15–17 kts by afternoon which could support some ridge soaring, but the combination of overcast, gusty winds (KRHV 12G19KT), and post-precipitation instability makes this a no-fly day.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: SSW 7–10 kts, BL only 265–380m, 100% cloud cover. Ridge level winds 9–14 kts SSW — technically on direction but the BL is far below summit elevation and no thermals are possible.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds build to 10–14 kts SSW, 80m winds 12–17 kts. CAPE 620–920 J/kg but fully overcast. BL reaches 505–735m — still well below the 767m summit. No viable flight.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 12–14 kts SSW with gusts, 80m winds 14–18 kts. A brief cloud break around 4 PM (29% cloud cover) is possible, but it is too late in the day and the air is post-frontal and gusty — not worth launching.

verdict.hazards: 100% overcast eliminating thermals, BL top below summit all day, gusty ridge-level winds, post-frontal instability with CAPE up to 920 J/kg under cloud

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Saturday looks windy and overcast again — HRRR shows 16–18 kts SSW at surface by midday, 80m winds reaching 22–26 kts. BL builds to 920m but cloud cover is 99–100%. Tomorrow.io shows gusts to 17–23 kts at the upper launch. Too windy and overcast for thermal flying. P3+ locals might probe late morning if clouds thin, but don't count on it.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Saturday winds at the lower launch 13–16 kts SSW with gusts to 20+ kts per Tomorrow.io. Way too strong for the training hill. NO FLY.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Saturday shows better wind direction — WSW to SW (195–225°) at 12–19 kts, building to 22+ kts by evening. Morning 8 AM looks cleanest with 7.7 kts at 207°, but 80m winds already at 12 kts. By midday winds reach 15–16 kts with 80m at 22–24 kts — getting into the strong/punchy range. Tomorrow.io shows gusts to 26 kts by 6 PM. P3+ experienced Mussel Rock pilots may find a window 10 AM–noon before it gets too strong. Watch for overdevelopment.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Saturday mirrors Ed Levin — 16–18 kts SSW at surface, 80m at 21–26 kts, 99–100% overcast. BL only reaches 920m against a 767m summit. Too strong and too cloudy. NO FLY.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Sunday (Apr 12) shows a marked improvement in cloud cover — partial clearing by noon (83% dropping) and possible BL heights of 1155–1720m by afternoon. Winds lighten to 6–9 kts SSW with westerly shift. CAPE 30–200 J/kg. This could be the first flyable day of the stretch if clouds clear — watch the morning precip (70–73% chance) burning off. Potentially MARGINAL to FLY by midday/early afternoon.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Sunday similar story to upper launch — morning clouds and precip chance, but afternoon clearing possible with BL 1155–1720m. Winds lightening to 6–9 kts with a westerly drift. Could be viable for a thermal session if the morning clears.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Sunday shows winds from the W-WNW (250–270°) at 10–12 kts — better direction for the ridge. BL 830–1390m, cloud cover clearing significantly by afternoon. If the W wind holds at 10–14 kts this could be a good ridge day. Precip risk drops from 75% morning to 18% evening. Worth a closer look Sunday morning.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Sunday shows clearing after a rainy morning (67–72% precip chance early). By afternoon, BL reaches 1145–1645m and winds lighten to 6–10 kts with a SW-W shift. If the morning rain clears, afternoon thermals are possible with CAPE 60–130 J/kg. Marginal but improving — monitor Saturday evening forecasts.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: Day 3 (Monday, Apr 13) is beyond reliable HRRR range. GFS data not available in this dataset — extrapolating from the trend of clearing post-Sunday, conditions may improve further. Check updated models Sunday evening.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Day 3 outlook mirrors upper launch — likely improving if the Sunday clearing trend continues. No reliable data; check Sunday evening for updated GFS/HRRR.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Day 3 no direct model data. If the high-pressure ridge rebuilds post-system, classic NW afternoon sea breeze should return. Typically excellent Mussel Rock conditions develop 2–3 days after frontal passage.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Day 3 outlook uncertain. Post-system clearing with rebuilding high pressure typically brings the best thermal days to Mission Peak. Check Monday AM for updated briefing.

watchlist: Marine layer: KHAF near-saturated (17/17°C dewpoint spread) with cold SST 14°C and 13s wave period — coastal sites may not clear at all Friday or Saturday
Post-frontal CAPE: 430–920 J/kg under overcast at Ed Levin and Mission Peak — if any breaks appear, thermals could be punchy and overdeveloped rapidly
Sunday clearing: 70–75% precip chance Sunday morning but afternoon models show BL 1155–1720m and partial clearing — this could be the first flyable day; monitor closely
Wind speed Saturday: HRRR showing 80m winds 22–26 kts at Ed Levin and Mission Peak Saturday — even if clouds thin, ridge-level flow likely too strong for safe flying
KRHV gusts: 12G19KT today confirms active gusty sea breeze push in the South Bay — any site in this corridor will have punchy, turbulent air
tldr: Friday is a wash at all four sites — post-frontal overcast (100% cloud cover all day) kills thermals at Ed Levin and Mission Peak, while Mussel Rock has wrong-direction southerly flow and a deep marine layer. Saturday stays strong and cloudy. Sunday is your first real shot, especially if morning rain clears by noon — check updated models Saturday evening for Mussel Rock and Ed Levin upper.
